Module contents

Univariate visualization charts, diagnostics, and statistical helpers.

class dataviz.univariate.BooleanRateInterval(true_rate: float, lower: float, upper: float, confidence_level: float)[source]

Bases: object

Wilson confidence interval for a boolean true rate.

Parameters:

  • true_rate (float) – Observed true rate.

  • lower (float) – Lower Wilson confidence bound.

  • upper (float) – Upper Wilson confidence bound.

  • confidence_level (float) – Confidence level.

Returns:

Immutable boolean rate interval.

Return type:

BooleanRateInterval

Raises:

  • TypeError – If fields cannot be represented by declared types.

  • ValueError – If interval bounds are invalid.

Examples

`python interval = BooleanRateInterval(0.6, 0.4, 0.8, 0.95) `

Notes

Wilson intervals behave better than Wald intervals near 0 or 1.

confidence_level: float
lower: float
true_rate: float
upper: float
class dataviz.univariate.BooleanSummary(count: int, true_count: int, false_count: int, true_rate: float)[source]

Bases: object

Boolean variable summary.

Parameters:

  • count (int) – Number of non-missing boolean observations.

  • true_count (int) – Number of true observations.

  • false_count (int) – Number of false observations.

  • true_rate (float) – Share of true observations.

Returns:

Immutable boolean summary.

Return type:

BooleanSummary

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by declared fields.

  • ValueError – If rates are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python summary = BooleanSummary(10, 7, 3, 0.7) `

Notes

Boolean summaries are useful for one-dimensional binary indicators.

count: int
false_count: int
true_count: int
true_rate: float
class dataviz.univariate.BootstrapCI(statistic: str, estimate: float, lower: float, upper: float, confidence_level: float, n_resamples: int)[source]

Bases: object

Bootstrap confidence interval result.

Parameters:

  • statistic (str) – Statistic name.

  • estimate (float) – Statistic computed on original data.

  • lower (float) – Lower confidence bound.

  • upper (float) – Upper confidence bound.

  • confidence_level (float) – Confidence level.

  • n_resamples (int) – Number of bootstrap resamples.

Returns:

Immutable bootstrap interval summary.

Return type:

BootstrapCI

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by declared fields.

  • ValueError – If interval values are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python ci = BootstrapCI("mean", 2.0, 1.5, 2.5, 0.95, 1000) `

Notes

Intervals use the percentile bootstrap method.

confidence_level: float
estimate: float
lower: float
n_resamples: int
statistic: str
upper: float
class dataviz.univariate.ConcentrationStats(total: float, gini: float, top_10_share: float, top_20_share: float)[source]

Bases: object

Concentration statistics for non-negative values.

Parameters:

  • total (float) – Sum of non-negative values.

  • gini (float) – Gini coefficient.

  • top_10_share (float) – Share contributed by the largest 10 percent of observations.

  • top_20_share (float) – Share contributed by the largest 20 percent of observations.

Returns:

Immutable inequality and concentration summary.

Return type:

ConcentrationStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If rates are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python stats = ConcentrationStats(100.0, 0.25, 0.4, 0.6) `

Notes

Concentration metrics assume non-negative values.

gini: float
top_10_share: float
top_20_share: float
total: float
class dataviz.univariate.DataQualitySummary(count: int, missing: int, missing_rate: float, unique: int, duplicate_rate: float, zero_rate: float, negative_rate: float)[source]

Bases: object

Univariate data quality summary.

Parameters:

  • count (int) – Total number of observations.

  • missing (int) – Number of missing observations.

  • missing_rate (float) – Share of observations that are missing.

  • unique (int) – Number of unique non-missing values.

  • duplicate_rate (float) – Share of non-missing observations that repeat a previous value.

  • zero_rate (float) – Share of numeric observations equal to zero.

  • negative_rate (float) – Share of numeric observations below zero.

Returns:

Immutable data quality profile.

Return type:

DataQualitySummary

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If rates are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python profile = DataQualitySummary(10, 1, 0.1, 4, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1) `

Notes

Numeric-only rates are zero when no numeric observations are available.

count: int
duplicate_rate: float
missing: int
missing_rate: float
negative_rate: float
unique: int
zero_rate: float
class dataviz.univariate.DistributionFit(distribution: str, parameters: Tuple[float, ...], statistic: float, p_value: float, aic: float, bic: float)[source]

Bases: object

Fitted SciPy distribution summary.

Parameters:

  • distribution (str) – Name of the fitted SciPy distribution.

  • parameters (Tuple[float, ...]) – Fitted distribution parameters.

  • statistic (float) – Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness-of-fit statistic.

  • p_value (float) – Kolmogorov-Smirnov p-value.

  • aic (float) – Akaike information criterion.

  • bic (float) – Bayesian information criterion.

Returns:

Immutable fitted distribution summary.

Return type:

DistributionFit

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by declared fields.

  • ValueError – If values are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python fit = DistributionFit("norm", (0.0, 1.0), 0.05, 0.9, 120.0, 124.0) `

Notes

Smaller AIC or BIC values indicate a better relative fit among candidate distributions.

aic: float
bic: float
distribution: str
p_value: float
parameters: Tuple[float, ...]
statistic: float
class dataviz.univariate.NormalityTestResult(statistic: float, p_value: float, method: str, is_normal: bool, alpha: float)[source]

Bases: object

Normality test result for one variable.

Parameters:

  • statistic (float) – Test statistic.

  • p_value (float) – Test p-value.

  • method (str) – Normality test method.

  • is_normal (bool) – Whether p_value is greater than or equal to alpha.

  • alpha (float) – Significance threshold used for the decision.

Returns:

Immutable normality test summary.

Return type:

NormalityTestResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented in the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If fields are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python result = NormalityTestResult(0.98, 0.42, "shapiro", True, 0.05) `

Notes

Statistical tests are most useful when paired with QQ or PP plots.

alpha: float
is_normal: bool
method: str
p_value: float
statistic: float
class dataviz.univariate.OutlierTreatmentResult(original: Series, treated: Series, mask: Series, method: str, rule: str)[source]

Bases: object

Result of an outlier treatment operation.

Parameters:

  • original (pandas.Series) – Cleaned original numeric observations.

  • treated (pandas.Series) – Treated observations.

  • mask (pandas.Series) – Boolean outlier mask.

  • method (str) – Treatment method name.

  • rule (str) – Outlier detection rule name.

Returns:

Immutable outlier treatment result.

Return type:

OutlierTreatmentResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If fields cannot be represented by declared types.

  • ValueError – If fields are inconsistent.

Examples

`python result = OutlierTreatmentResult(original, treated, mask, "cap", "iqr") `

Notes

The result preserves both original and treated values for auditability.

mask: Series
method: str
original: Series
rule: str
treated: Series
class dataviz.univariate.RobustStats(count: int, median: float, mad: float, scaled_mad: float, trimmed_mean: float, winsorized_mean: float, q1: float, q3: float, iqr: float, lower_fence: float, upper_fence: float)[source]

Bases: object

Robust summary statistics for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • count (int) – Number of non-missing numeric observations.

  • median (float) – Median value.

  • mad (float) – Median absolute deviation from the median.

  • scaled_mad (float) – MAD scaled by 1.4826 for normal consistency.

  • trimmed_mean (float) – Mean after trimming both tails.

  • winsorized_mean (float) – Mean after winsorizing both tails.

  • q1 (float) – First quartile.

  • q3 (float) – Third quartile.

  • iqr (float) – Interquartile range.

  • lower_fence (float) – Tukey lower outlier fence.

  • upper_fence (float) – Tukey upper outlier fence.

Returns:

Immutable robust summary record.

Return type:

RobustStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If values are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python summary = RobustStats(5, 3.0, 1.0, 1.4826, 3.0, 3.0, 2.0, 4.0, 2.0, -1.0, 7.0) `

Notes

Robust summaries are less sensitive to heavy tails and isolated extreme observations.

count: int
iqr: float
lower_fence: float
mad: float
median: float
q1: float
q3: float
scaled_mad: float
trimmed_mean: float
upper_fence: float
winsorized_mean: float
class dataviz.univariate.TransformResult(values: Series, method: str, parameter: float | None)[source]

Bases: object

Transformed univariate data and transformation metadata.

Parameters:

  • values (pandas.Series) – Transformed observations.

  • method (str) – Transformation method name.

  • parameter (Optional[float]) – Fitted transformation parameter when applicable.

Returns:

Immutable transformation result.

Return type:

TransformResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If transformation metadata is invalid.

Examples

`python result = TransformResult(pd.Series([0.0, 1.0]), "log", None) `

Notes

Box-Cox and Yeo-Johnson parameters are estimated by SciPy.

method: str
parameter: float | None
values: Series
class dataviz.univariate.UnivariateInput(values: Series, name: str, kind: str, missing_count: int)[source]

Bases: object

Resolved univariate input data.

Parameters:

  • values (pandas.Series) – Resolved observations.

  • name (str) – Display name for the variable.

  • kind (str) – Inferred variable kind.

  • missing_count (int) – Number of missing values in the original input.

Returns:

Immutable resolved input summary.

Return type:

UnivariateInput

Raises:

  • TypeError – If fields cannot be represented by the declared types.

  • ValueError – If fields are inconsistent with downstream use.

Examples

`python resolved = UnivariateInput(pd.Series([1, 2]), "sales", "numeric", 0) `

Notes

This dataclass is intended for wrappers and future API-normalization work.

kind: str
missing_count: int
name: str
values: Series
class dataviz.univariate.UnivariateProfile(name: str, kind: str, quality: DataQualitySummary, summary: Dict[str, object])[source]

Bases: object

Automatic univariate profile.

Parameters:

  • name (str) – Variable name.

  • kind (str) – Inferred variable kind.

  • quality (DataQualitySummary) – Data quality summary.

  • summary (Dict[str, object]) – Type-specific summary payload.

Returns:

Immutable automatic profile.

Return type:

UnivariateProfile

Raises:

  • TypeError – If fields cannot be represented by declared types.

  • ValueError – If profile values are inconsistent.

Examples

`python profile = UnivariateProfile("sales", "numeric", quality, {"stats": stats}) `

Notes

The summary payload intentionally stays dictionary-based for mixed variable kinds.

kind: str
name: str
quality: DataQualitySummary
summary: Dict[str, object]
class dataviz.univariate.UnivariateStats(count: int, missing: int, mean: float, median: float, std: float, variance: float, minimum: float, q1: float, q3: float, maximum: float, iqr: float, skewness: float, kurtosis: float, sem: float, mad: float)[source]

Bases: object

Summary statistics for one variable.

Parameters:

  • count (int) – Number of non-missing observations.

  • missing (int) – Number of missing observations removed from the summary.

  • mean (float) – Arithmetic mean.

  • median (float) – Median value.

  • std (float) – Sample standard deviation.

  • variance (float) – Sample variance.

  • minimum (float) – Minimum observed value.

  • q1 (float) – First quartile.

  • q3 (float) – Third quartile.

  • maximum (float) – Maximum observed value.

  • iqr (float) – Interquartile range.

  • skewness (float) – Fisher-Pearson skewness estimate.

  • kurtosis (float) – Fisher kurtosis estimate.

  • sem (float) – Standard error of the mean.

  • mad (float) – Median absolute deviation from the median.

Returns:

Immutable univariate summary record.

Return type:

UnivariateStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If provided values cannot be represented by the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If a statistic is incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python stats = UnivariateStats(10, 0, 5.0, 5.0, 1.0, 1.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0, 7.0, 2.0, 0.0, -1.2, 0.3, 1.0) `

Notes

The dataclass is returned by univariate_summary and can be converted with dataclasses.asdict.

count: int
iqr: float
kurtosis: float
mad: float
maximum: float
mean: float
median: float
minimum: float
missing: int
q1: float
q3: float
sem: float
skewness: float
std: float
variance: float
class dataviz.univariate.WeightedStats(count: int, weight_sum: float, mean: float, variance: float, std: float, q1: float, median: float, q3: float)[source]

Bases: object

Weighted summary statistics for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • count (int) – Number of observations after missing-value removal.

  • weight_sum (float) – Sum of non-negative weights.

  • mean (float) – Weighted arithmetic mean.

  • variance (float) – Weighted population variance.

  • std (float) – Weighted population standard deviation.

  • q1 (float) – Weighted first quartile.

  • median (float) – Weighted median.

  • q3 (float) – Weighted third quartile.

Returns:

Immutable weighted summary record.

Return type:

WeightedStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented by the declared fields.

  • ValueError – If summary values are incompatible with downstream numeric use.

Examples

`python stats = WeightedStats(3, 6.0, 2.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0) `

Notes

Weighted summaries are useful for survey data, grouped data, and importance-weighted samples.

count: int
mean: float
median: float
q1: float
q3: float
std: float
variance: float
weight_sum: float
dataviz.univariate.as_datetime_series(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, name: str = 'Timestamp') → Series[source]

Convert series-like values to a clean datetime Series.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • name (str) – Fallback series name.

Returns:

Datetime observations sorted ascending.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If no valid datetime observations remain.

Examples

`python timestamps = as_datetime_series(["2026-01-01", "2026-01-02"]) `

Notes

Missing and unparseable timestamps are removed after coercion.

dataviz.univariate.as_numeric_series(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, name: str = 'Value') → Series[source]

Convert series-like data to a clean numeric pandas Series.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • name (str) – Fallback series name for unnamed arrays.

Returns:

Numeric observations with missing values removed.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be coerced to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If no numeric observations remain.

Examples

`python values = as_numeric_series([1, 2, 3]) `

Notes

This helper is intentionally shared by visual and non-visual univariate utilities.

dataviz.univariate.auto_profile(value: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, name: str = 'Value') → UnivariateProfile[source]

Create an automatic type-aware profile for one variable.

Parameters:

  • value (SeriesLike) – Series-like values or a dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pandas.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column-name lookup.

  • name (str) – Fallback name for unnamed arrays.

Returns:

Quality metrics and type-specific summary information.

Return type:

UnivariateProfile

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be resolved.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python profile = auto_profile("sales", data=df) `

Notes

Numeric variables include descriptive statistics; boolean variables include true-rate summary.

dataviz.univariate.auto_profile_chart(value: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, name: str = 'Value', title: str | None = None) → Figure

Create an interactive chart selected from inferred variable type.

Parameters:

  • value (SeriesLike) – Series-like values or a dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pandas.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column-name lookup.

  • name (str) – Fallback name for unnamed arrays.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

Returns:

Type-appropriate interactive profile chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be resolved.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or a chart cannot be selected.

Examples

`python fig = auto_profile_chart_interactive("sales", data=df) `

Notes

Numeric values use the univariate dashboard; categorical values use a frequency bar.

dataviz.univariate.auto_profile_chart_interactive(value: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, name: str = 'Value', title: str | None = None) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive chart selected from inferred variable type.

Parameters:

  • value (SeriesLike) – Series-like values or a dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pandas.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column-name lookup.

  • name (str) – Fallback name for unnamed arrays.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

Returns:

Type-appropriate interactive profile chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be resolved.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or a chart cannot be selected.

Examples

`python fig = auto_profile_chart_interactive("sales", data=df) `

Notes

Numeric values use the univariate dashboard; categorical values use a frequency bar.

dataviz.univariate.boolean_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (6, 5), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static true/false count chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input boolean-like observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing true/false counts.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = boolean_bar_static(flags) `

Notes

This is a semantic wrapper around categorical frequency bars.

dataviz.univariate.boolean_bar_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 500, width: int = 700) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive true/false count chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input boolean-like observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive true/false count chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = boolean_bar_interactive(flags) `

Notes

This is a semantic wrapper around categorical frequency bars.

dataviz.univariate.boolean_bar_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (6, 5), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static true/false count chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input boolean-like observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing true/false counts.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = boolean_bar_static(flags) `

Notes

This is a semantic wrapper around categorical frequency bars.

dataviz.univariate.boolean_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → BooleanSummary[source]

Summarize a boolean-like variable.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input boolean-like observations.

Returns:

Counts and true-rate summary.

Return type:

BooleanSummary

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python summary = boolean_summary([True, False, True]) `

Notes

Values are coerced with pandas boolean semantics after missing values are removed.

dataviz.univariate.boolean_wilson_interval(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, confidence_level: float = 0.95) → BooleanRateInterval[source]

Compute a Wilson confidence interval for a boolean true rate.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input boolean-like observations.

  • confidence_level (float) – Confidence level in the open (0, 1) interval.

Returns:

Observed rate and Wilson interval bounds.

Return type:

BooleanRateInterval

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If confidence level is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python interval = boolean_wilson_interval(flags) `

Notes

The z value is computed from the normal quantile function.

dataviz.univariate.bootstrap_ci(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median', 'std'] = 'mean', confidence_level: float = 0.95, n_resamples: int = 1000, seed: int | None = None) → BootstrapCI[source]

Compute a percentile bootstrap confidence interval.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • statistic (StatisticName) – Statistic to bootstrap.

  • confidence_level (float) – Confidence level in the open (0, 1) interval.

  • n_resamples (int) – Number of bootstrap samples.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducibility.

Returns:

Original estimate and bootstrap confidence interval.

Return type:

BootstrapCI

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If options are invalid.

Examples

`python ci = bootstrap_ci(data, statistic="mean") `

Notes

The percentile method is simple, stable, and dependency-light.

dataviz.univariate.bootstrap_distribution(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median', 'std'] = 'mean', n_resamples: int = 1000, seed: int | None = None) → Series[source]

Generate a bootstrap distribution for one statistic.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • statistic (StatisticName) – Statistic to bootstrap.

  • n_resamples (int) – Number of bootstrap samples.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducibility.

Returns:

Bootstrap statistic values.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If statistic or n_resamples is invalid.

Examples

`python samples = bootstrap_distribution(data, statistic="median") `

Notes

Bootstrap samples are drawn with replacement and the same size as the observed data.

dataviz.univariate.bootstrap_distribution_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median', 'std'] = 'mean', n_resamples: int = 1000, seed: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None) → Axes

Create a static histogram of bootstrap statistic values.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • statistic (StatisticName) – Statistic to bootstrap.

  • n_resamples (int) – Number of bootstrap samples.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducibility.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Histogram color.

Returns:

Axes containing the bootstrap distribution.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If options are invalid.

Examples

`python ax = bootstrap_distribution_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The original-sample statistic is shown as a vertical reference line.

dataviz.univariate.bootstrap_distribution_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median', 'std'] = 'mean', n_resamples: int = 1000, seed: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram of bootstrap statistic values.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • statistic (StatisticName) – Statistic to bootstrap.

  • n_resamples (int) – Number of bootstrap samples.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducibility.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Histogram color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive bootstrap distribution.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If options are invalid.

Examples

`python fig = bootstrap_distribution_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

The original-sample statistic is shown as a vertical reference line.

dataviz.univariate.bootstrap_distribution_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median', 'std'] = 'mean', n_resamples: int = 1000, seed: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None) → Axes[source]

Create a static histogram of bootstrap statistic values.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • statistic (StatisticName) – Statistic to bootstrap.

  • n_resamples (int) – Number of bootstrap samples.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducibility.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Histogram color.

Returns:

Axes containing the bootstrap distribution.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If options are invalid.

Examples

`python ax = bootstrap_distribution_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The original-sample statistic is shown as a vertical reference line.

dataviz.univariate.box_plot(data: DataFrame | ndarray | Sequence[Sequence[float]], title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, patch_artist: bool = True, notch: bool = False, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', widths: float = 0.6, **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static box plot that summarizes quartiles, spread, and outliers.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (FrameLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • patch_artist (bool) – Configuration value for patch_artist. Defaults to True.

  • notch (bool) – Configuration value for notch. Defaults to False.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • widths (float) – Configuration value for widths. Defaults to 0.6.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.box_plot_static(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.box_plot_interactive(data: DataFrame | ndarray | Sequence[Sequence[float]], title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'closest', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, boxmean: bool = True, points: str = 'outliers', **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive box plot that summarizes quartiles, spread, and outliers.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (FrameLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for marker_color. Defaults to None.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'closest'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 1000.

  • boxmean (bool) – Configuration value for boxmean. Defaults to True.

  • points (str) – Configuration value for points. Defaults to 'outliers'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.box_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.box_plot_static(data: DataFrame | ndarray | Sequence[Sequence[float]], title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, patch_artist: bool = True, notch: bool = False, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', widths: float = 0.6, **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static box plot that summarizes quartiles, spread, and outliers.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (FrameLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • patch_artist (bool) – Configuration value for patch_artist. Defaults to True.

  • notch (bool) – Configuration value for notch. Defaults to False.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • widths (float) – Configuration value for widths. Defaults to 0.6.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.box_plot_static(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.cap_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5) → OutlierTreatmentResult[source]

Cap outliers to non-outlier min and max values.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule: "iqr", "zscore", or "mad".

  • threshold (float) – Threshold for z-score or MAD rules.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Original values, capped values, and mask.

Return type:

OutlierTreatmentResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If all observations are outliers or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python result = cap_outliers(values) `

Notes

Capping preserves sample size while limiting extreme values.

dataviz.univariate.category_counts(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: bool = False, dropna: bool = True, sort: bool = True, top_n: int | None = None) → Series[source]

Compute category counts or proportions for one variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to return proportions instead of counts.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

  • sort (bool) – Whether to sort categories by frequency descending.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of most common categories to keep.

Returns:

Category counts or proportions indexed by category label.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If top_n is provided and is not positive, or no categories remain.

Examples

`python counts = category_counts(["A", "B", "A"]) `

Notes

This helper underpins categorical bar and Pareto chart functions.

dataviz.univariate.compare_distributions(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distributions: Iterable[str] = ('norm', 'lognorm', 'expon', 'gamma', 'weibull_min')) → DataFrame[source]

Fit and rank multiple candidate distributions.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distributions (Iterable[str]) – Candidate SciPy distribution names.

Returns:

Fit metrics sorted by AIC ascending.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no candidate distributions are provided or fitting fails.

Examples

`python table = compare_distributions(data, ["norm", "gamma"]) `

Notes

The parameter tuple is retained so callers can reproduce the fitted distribution.

dataviz.univariate.concentration_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → ConcentrationStats[source]

Compute Gini and top-share concentration statistics.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

Returns:

Total, Gini coefficient, and top-share metrics.

Return type:

ConcentrationStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python summary = concentration_summary(revenue) `

Notes

Values are sorted ascending for Gini and descending for top-share calculations.

dataviz.univariate.cumulative_histogram(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Cumulative Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.7, density: bool = False, theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static cumulative histogram for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • alpha (float) – Bar opacity.

  • density (bool) – Whether to normalize cumulative counts to density.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.hist.

Returns:

Axes containing the cumulative histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If bins is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = cumulative_histogram_static(data, bins=20) `

Notes

Cumulative histograms emphasize accumulation across a measurement scale.

dataviz.univariate.cumulative_histogram_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Cumulative Count', color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.7, histnorm: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive cumulative histogram for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • alpha (float) – Bar opacity.

  • histnorm (Optional[str]) – Optional Plotly histogram normalization.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Histogram.

Returns:

Interactive cumulative histogram.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If bins is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = cumulative_histogram_interactive(data) `

Notes

Plotly handles cumulative aggregation through the trace cumulative option.

dataviz.univariate.cumulative_histogram_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Cumulative Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.7, density: bool = False, theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static cumulative histogram for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • alpha (float) – Bar opacity.

  • density (bool) – Whether to normalize cumulative counts to density.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.hist.

Returns:

Axes containing the cumulative histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If bins is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = cumulative_histogram_static(data, bins=20) `

Notes

Cumulative histograms emphasize accumulation across a measurement scale.

dataviz.univariate.data_quality_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → DataQualitySummary[source]

Compute basic data quality metrics for one variable.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

Returns:

Missingness, uniqueness, duplicate, zero, and negative rates.

Return type:

DataQualitySummary

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the input has no observations.

Examples

`python summary = data_quality_summary(series) `

Notes

This summary is intentionally type-tolerant so it works for numeric and categorical variables.

dataviz.univariate.density_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2, alpha: float = 0.7, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'x unified', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, histnorm: str = 'probability density', **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive density chart for one variable.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 2.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'x unified'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 1000.

  • histnorm (str) – Configuration value for histnorm. Defaults to 'probability density'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.density_interactive(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.density_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.7, fill: bool = True, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static kernel density estimate chart for one variable.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 2.0.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • fill (bool) – Configuration value for fill. Defaults to True.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.density_static(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.density_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.7, fill: bool = True, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static kernel density estimate chart for one variable.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 2.0.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • fill (bool) – Configuration value for fill. Defaults to True.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.density_static(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.dot_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Count', ylabel: str = 'Category', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, top_n: int | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static Cleveland dot plot for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the dot plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = dot_plot_static(categories) `

Notes

Dot plots are quieter than bars when comparing many category counts.

dataviz.univariate.dot_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Count', ylabel: str = 'Category', color: str | None = None, top_n: int | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive Cleveland dot plot for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive dot plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = dot_plot_interactive(categories) `

Notes

The sorted y-axis keeps small and large categories easy to compare.

dataviz.univariate.dot_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Count', ylabel: str = 'Category', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, top_n: int | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static Cleveland dot plot for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the dot plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = dot_plot_static(categories) `

Notes

Dot plots are quieter than bars when comparing many category counts.

dataviz.univariate.ecdf_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Cumulative Probability', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, marker: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default', grid: bool = True, **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static empirical cumulative distribution plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • marker (Optional[str]) – Optional marker style.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show grid lines.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.step.

Returns:

Axes containing the ECDF chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing numeric observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = ecdf_plot_static(data) `

Notes

ECDF plots avoid binning and are useful for comparing distribution tails.

dataviz.univariate.ecdf_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Cumulative Probability', color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive empirical cumulative distribution plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive ECDF chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing numeric observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = ecdf_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

The line shape uses Plotly’s horizontal-vertical step mode.

dataviz.univariate.ecdf_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Cumulative Probability', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, marker: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default', grid: bool = True, **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static empirical cumulative distribution plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • marker (Optional[str]) – Optional marker style.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show grid lines.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.step.

Returns:

Axes containing the ECDF chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing numeric observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = ecdf_plot_static(data) `

Notes

ECDF plots avoid binning and are useful for comparing distribution tails.

dataviz.univariate.ecdf_values(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute empirical cumulative distribution function values.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

Returns:

Sorted values and cumulative probabilities.

Return type:

tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing numeric observations remain.

Examples

`python x, y = ecdf_values([3, 1, 2]) `

Notes

Probabilities use the conventional rank / n plotting positions.

dataviz.univariate.event_counts(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, freq: Literal['D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'Y', 'H'] = 'D') → Series[source]

Count datetime events per calendar frequency.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • freq (DatetimeFreq) – Pandas resampling frequency.

Returns:

Event counts indexed by period start timestamp.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If no valid datetime observations remain.

Examples

`python counts = event_counts(timestamps, freq="W") `

Notes

The result includes empty periods between the first and last observation.

dataviz.univariate.event_frequency_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, freq: Literal['D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'Y', 'H'] = 'D', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Time', ylabel: str = 'Event Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static event frequency chart from datetime observations.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • freq (DatetimeFreq) – Pandas resampling frequency.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the event frequency chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If no valid datetime observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = event_frequency_plot_static(timestamps, freq="M") `

Notes

This view is useful for one-dimensional event streams.

dataviz.univariate.event_frequency_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, freq: Literal['D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'Y', 'H'] = 'D', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Time', ylabel: str = 'Event Count', color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive event frequency chart from datetime observations.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • freq (DatetimeFreq) – Pandas resampling frequency.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive event frequency chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If no valid datetime observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = event_frequency_plot_interactive(timestamps) `

Notes

Hover labels expose period-level event counts.

dataviz.univariate.event_frequency_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, freq: Literal['D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'Y', 'H'] = 'D', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Time', ylabel: str = 'Event Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static event frequency chart from datetime observations.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • freq (DatetimeFreq) – Pandas resampling frequency.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the event frequency chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If no valid datetime observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = event_frequency_plot_static(timestamps, freq="M") `

Notes

This view is useful for one-dimensional event streams.

dataviz.univariate.exceedance_table(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, thresholds: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → DataFrame[source]

Compute exceedance counts and rates for thresholds.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • thresholds (SeriesLike) – Threshold values.

Returns:

Threshold, exceedance count, and exceedance rate columns.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data or thresholds cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If no observations or thresholds remain.

Examples

`python table = exceedance_table(values, [10, 20, 30]) `

Notes

Exceedance means strictly greater than the threshold.

dataviz.univariate.fit_distribution(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm') → DistributionFit[source]

Fit a SciPy continuous distribution to one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name.

Returns:

Fitted parameters and goodness-of-fit metrics.

Return type:

DistributionFit

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the distribution name is invalid or fitting fails.

Examples

`python fit = fit_distribution(data, distribution="lognorm") `

Notes

AIC and BIC are computed from the fitted log-likelihood.

dataviz.univariate.fitted_distribution_histogram(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, fit_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static histogram with a fitted probability density overlay.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • fit_color (str) – Fitted density line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.hist.

Returns:

Axes containing the histogram and fitted density.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If bins is not positive or fitting fails.

Examples

`python ax = fitted_distribution_histogram_static(data, distribution="gamma") `

Notes

The histogram is density-normalized so the fitted PDF is on the same scale.

dataviz.univariate.fitted_distribution_histogram_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, fit_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram with a fitted probability density overlay.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • fit_color (str) – Fitted density line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Histogram.

Returns:

Interactive histogram and fitted density.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If bins is not positive or fitting fails.

Examples

`python fig = fitted_distribution_histogram_interactive(data) `

Notes

The histogram uses probability-density normalization for visual comparability.

dataviz.univariate.fitted_distribution_histogram_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, fit_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static histogram with a fitted probability density overlay.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • fit_color (str) – Fitted density line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.hist.

Returns:

Axes containing the histogram and fitted density.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If bins is not positive or fitting fails.

Examples

`python ax = fitted_distribution_histogram_static(data, distribution="gamma") `

Notes

The histogram is density-normalized so the fitted PDF is on the same scale.

dataviz.univariate.fitted_pdf_values(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', points: int = 200) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray, DistributionFit][source]

Compute fitted probability density curve values.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name.

  • points (int) – Number of curve points to generate.

Returns:

X values, PDF values, and fit summary.

Return type:

Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray, DistributionFit]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If points is not positive or fitting fails.

Examples

`python x, y, fit = fitted_pdf_values(data, "norm") `

Notes

The curve spans the observed data range.

dataviz.univariate.flag_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5) → DataFrame[source]

Return values with an explicit outlier flag.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule: "iqr", "zscore", or "mad".

  • threshold (float) – Threshold for z-score or MAD rules.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Value and outlier flag columns.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python frame = flag_outliers(values) `

Notes

Flagging is often preferable when downstream users need transparency.

dataviz.univariate.frequency_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: bool = False, dropna: bool = True, sort: bool = True, top_n: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, rotation: int = 45, theme: str = 'default', grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static categorical frequency bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

  • sort (bool) – Whether to sort categories by frequency descending.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of most common categories to show.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • rotation (int) – X tick label rotation in degrees.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show y-axis grid lines.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Grid opacity.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.bar.

Returns:

Axes containing the frequency chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = frequency_bar_static(["A", "B", "A"]) `

Notes

Use normalize=True when relative frequency is more meaningful than count.

dataviz.univariate.frequency_bar_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: bool = False, dropna: bool = True, sort: bool = True, top_n: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive categorical frequency bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

  • sort (bool) – Whether to sort categories by frequency descending.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of most common categories to show.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Bar.

Returns:

Interactive frequency chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = frequency_bar_interactive(["A", "B", "A"]) `

Notes

Hover labels show the category and frequency value.

dataviz.univariate.frequency_bar_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: bool = False, dropna: bool = True, sort: bool = True, top_n: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, rotation: int = 45, theme: str = 'default', grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static categorical frequency bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

  • sort (bool) – Whether to sort categories by frequency descending.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of most common categories to show.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • rotation (int) – X tick label rotation in degrees.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show y-axis grid lines.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Grid opacity.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.bar.

Returns:

Axes containing the frequency chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = frequency_bar_static(["A", "B", "A"]) `

Notes

Use normalize=True when relative frequency is more meaningful than count.

dataviz.univariate.histogram(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, edgecolor: str = 'black', alpha: float = 0.7, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static histogram that summarizes the frequency distribution of one variable.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used for histogram-like displays. Defaults to 30.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • edgecolor (str) – Configuration value for edgecolor. Defaults to 'black'.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.histogram_static(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.histogram_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.7, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'x unified', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, bargap: float = 0.1, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram that summarizes the frequency distribution of one variable.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used for histogram-like displays. Defaults to 30.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for marker_color. Defaults to None.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'x unified'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 1000.

  • bargap (float) – Configuration value for bargap. Defaults to 0.1.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.histogram_interactive(data) `

Notes
