Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.histogram_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, edgecolor: str = 'black', alpha: float = 0.7, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static histogram that summarizes the frequency distribution of one variable.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations, measurements, or values used to build the chart.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used for histogram-like displays. Defaults to 30.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • edgecolor (str) – Configuration value for edgecolor. Defaults to 'black'.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Examples

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.histogram_static(data) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.univariate.infer_univariate_kind(values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Literal['numeric', 'categorical', 'datetime', 'boolean', 'text'][source]

Infer a practical univariate data kind.

Parameters:

values (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

Returns:

One of "numeric", "categorical", "datetime", "boolean", or "text".

Return type:

UnivariateKind

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python kind = infer_univariate_kind([1, 2, 3]) `

Notes

Object data with low cardinality is treated as categorical; otherwise it is treated as text.

dataviz.univariate.interarrival_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, unit: str = 'D', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static histogram of interarrival times.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • unit (str) – Output unit for elapsed times.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the interarrival histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two datetime observations are available.

Examples

`python ax = interarrival_plot_static(timestamps) `

Notes

Interarrival plots reveal clustering or regularity in event streams.

dataviz.univariate.interarrival_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, unit: str = 'D', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram of interarrival times.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • unit (str) – Output unit for elapsed times.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive interarrival histogram.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two datetime observations are available.

Examples

`python fig = interarrival_plot_interactive(timestamps) `

Notes

The x-axis unit should be chosen to match the event process scale.

dataviz.univariate.interarrival_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, unit: str = 'D', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static histogram of interarrival times.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • unit (str) – Output unit for elapsed times.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the interarrival histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two datetime observations are available.

Examples

`python ax = interarrival_plot_static(timestamps) `

Notes

Interarrival plots reveal clustering or regularity in event streams.

dataviz.univariate.interarrival_times(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, unit: str = 'D') → Series[source]

Compute elapsed time between consecutive datetime observations.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input datetime-like observations.

  • unit (str) – Output unit accepted by pandas timedeltas, such as "D" or "h".

Returns:

Interarrival durations in the requested unit.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to datetimes.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two datetime observations are available.

Examples

`python gaps = interarrival_times(timestamps, unit="h") `

Notes

Durations are computed after sorting timestamps ascending.

dataviz.univariate.iqr_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, multiplier: float = 1.5) → Series[source]

Flag observations outside Tukey IQR fences.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier used to define lower and upper fences.

Returns:

Boolean mask where True marks an outlier.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If multiplier is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python mask = iqr_outliers([1, 2, 3, 100]) `

Notes

The returned mask is aligned to the cleaned non-missing numeric series.

dataviz.univariate.likert_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] = ('Strongly disagree', 'Disagree', 'Neutral', 'Agree', 'Strongly agree')) → DataFrame[source]

Summarize one Likert-style variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input Likert responses.

  • order (Sequence[str]) – Ordered response labels.

Returns:

Response, count, proportion, and cumulative proportion columns.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the response order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python table = likert_summary(responses) `

Notes

The default order follows a five-point agreement scale.

dataviz.univariate.lollipop_chart(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Category', ylabel: str = 'Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, stem_color: str = 'gray', top_n: int | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static lollipop chart for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot color.

  • stem_color (str) – Stem line color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the lollipop chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = lollipop_chart_static(categories) `

Notes

Lollipop charts reduce visual weight compared with full-width bars.

dataviz.univariate.lollipop_chart_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Category', ylabel: str = 'Count', color: str | None = None, top_n: int | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive lollipop chart for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot and stem color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive lollipop chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = lollipop_chart_interactive(categories) `

Notes

The line trace draws stems and the marker trace draws the lollipop heads.

dataviz.univariate.lollipop_chart_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Category', ylabel: str = 'Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, stem_color: str = 'gray', top_n: int | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static lollipop chart for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot color.

  • stem_color (str) – Stem line color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the lollipop chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = lollipop_chart_static(categories) `

Notes

Lollipop charts reduce visual weight compared with full-width bars.

dataviz.univariate.lorenz_curve(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'gray', theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static Lorenz curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Curve color.

  • reference_color (str) – Equality reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the Lorenz curve.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python ax = lorenz_curve_static(values) `

Notes

The farther the curve lies below equality, the higher the concentration.

dataviz.univariate.lorenz_curve_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'gray', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive Lorenz curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Curve color.

  • reference_color (str) – Equality reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive Lorenz curve.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python fig = lorenz_curve_interactive(values) `

Notes

The equality reference line represents perfectly even distribution.

dataviz.univariate.lorenz_curve_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'gray', theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static Lorenz curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Curve color.

  • reference_color (str) – Equality reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the Lorenz curve.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python ax = lorenz_curve_static(values) `

Notes

The farther the curve lies below equality, the higher the concentration.

dataviz.univariate.lorenz_curve_values(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute Lorenz curve coordinates for non-negative values.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

Returns:

Cumulative population share and cumulative value share.

Return type:

tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python population, value_share = lorenz_curve_values(values) `

Notes

The curve starts at (0, 0) and ends at (1, 1).

dataviz.univariate.mad_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, threshold: float = 3.5) → Series[source]

Flag observations with large robust MAD z-scores.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • threshold (float) – Absolute MAD z-score threshold.

Returns:

Boolean mask where True marks a robust outlier.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If threshold is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python mask = mad_outliers([1, 2, 3, 100]) `

Notes

MAD outliers are useful when the mean and standard deviation are distorted by extremes.

dataviz.univariate.mad_zscores(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, scale: float = 1.4826) → Series[source]

Compute robust z-scores using the median absolute deviation.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • scale (float) – MAD scaling constant.

Returns:

Robust z-scores aligned to cleaned numeric observations.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If scale is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python scores = mad_zscores([1, 2, 3, 100]) `

Notes

A zero MAD returns zeros because robust spread cannot be estimated.

dataviz.univariate.normality_test(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['shapiro', 'normaltest'] = 'shapiro', alpha: float = 0.05) → NormalityTestResult[source]

Run a normality test for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (NormalityMethod) – Test method, either "shapiro" or "normaltest".

  • alpha (float) – Significance threshold used for the normality decision.

Returns:

Statistic, p-value, method, and decision.

Return type:

NormalityTestResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If method or alpha is invalid, or too few observations are available.

Examples

`python result = normality_test(data, method="shapiro") `

Notes

Shapiro-Wilk requires at least three observations; D’Agostino-Pearson requires at least eight.

dataviz.univariate.numeric_or_none(values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Series | None[source]

Try to coerce values to numeric data.

Parameters:

values (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

Returns:

Numeric values when coercion leaves at least one observation, otherwise None.

Return type:

Optional[pandas.Series]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no meaningful conversion can be attempted.

Examples

`python numeric = numeric_or_none(series) `

Notes

This helper is permissive and is intended for profile functions, not strict plotting APIs.

dataviz.univariate.ordered_category_counts(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, dropna: bool = True) → Series[source]

Compute category counts in a meaningful order.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to return proportions instead of counts.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

Returns:

Ordered category counts or proportions.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python counts = ordered_category_counts(responses, order=["Low", "Medium", "High"]) `

Notes

Without order, categories are sorted by their natural label order.

dataviz.univariate.ordinal_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static ordered category bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the ordered bar chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python ax = ordinal_bar_static(ratings, order=["Low", "Medium", "High"]) `

Notes

Ordered bars avoid misleading frequency sorting for ordinal variables.

dataviz.univariate.ordinal_bar_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive ordered category bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive ordered bar chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python fig = ordinal_bar_interactive(ratings) `

Notes

The function keeps the caller-supplied order on the x-axis.

dataviz.univariate.ordinal_bar_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static ordered category bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the ordered bar chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python ax = ordinal_bar_static(ratings, order=["Low", "Medium", "High"]) `

Notes

Ordered bars avoid misleading frequency sorting for ordinal variables.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_mask(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5) → Series[source]

Compute an outlier mask using a named rule.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule: "iqr", "zscore", or "mad".

  • threshold (float) – Threshold for z-score or MAD rules.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Boolean mask where True marks outliers.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the rule or thresholds are invalid.

Examples

`python mask = outlier_mask(values, rule="mad") `

Notes

This helper provides a shared rule interface for cap, remove, and flag workflows.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['iqr', 'zscore'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Observation', ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, outlier_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static index plot that highlights univariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (OutlierMethod) – Outlier rule, either "iqr" or "zscore".

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold when method="zscore".

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier when method="iqr".

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Regular point color.

  • outlier_color (str) – Highlight color for outlier points.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the outlier diagnostic plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the selected outlier rule or threshold is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = outlier_plot_static(data, method="iqr") `

Notes

The x-axis is the cleaned observation order after missing values are removed.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['iqr', 'zscore'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Observation', ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, outlier_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive index plot that highlights univariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (OutlierMethod) – Outlier rule, either "iqr" or "zscore".

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold when method="zscore".

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier when method="iqr".

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Regular point color.

  • outlier_color (str) – Highlight color for outlier points.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive outlier diagnostic plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the selected outlier rule or threshold is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = outlier_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Hover labels make it easy to inspect extreme observations by index.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['iqr', 'zscore'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Observation', ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, outlier_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static index plot that highlights univariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (OutlierMethod) – Outlier rule, either "iqr" or "zscore".

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold when method="zscore".

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier when method="iqr".

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Regular point color.

  • outlier_color (str) – Highlight color for outlier points.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the outlier diagnostic plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the selected outlier rule or threshold is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = outlier_plot_static(data, method="iqr") `

Notes

The x-axis is the cleaned observation order after missing values are removed.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_treatment_comparison(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', treatment: Literal['cap', 'remove'] = 'cap', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static before-after box plot for outlier treatment.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule used for treatment.

  • treatment (Literal["cap", "remove"]) – Treatment method.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Box color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing before-after treatment comparison.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If treatment or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python ax = outlier_treatment_comparison_static(values) `

Notes

This plot is intended for treatment review, not automatic data cleaning approval.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_treatment_comparison_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', treatment: Literal['cap', 'remove'] = 'cap', title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive before-after box plot for outlier treatment.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule used for treatment.

  • treatment (Literal["cap", "remove"]) – Treatment method.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Box color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive before-after treatment comparison.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If treatment or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python fig = outlier_treatment_comparison_interactive(values) `

Notes

Hover labels expose original and treated distributions side by side.

dataviz.univariate.outlier_treatment_comparison_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', treatment: Literal['cap', 'remove'] = 'cap', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static before-after box plot for outlier treatment.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule used for treatment.

  • treatment (Literal["cap", "remove"]) – Treatment method.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Box color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing before-after treatment comparison.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If treatment or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python ax = outlier_treatment_comparison_static(values) `

Notes

This plot is intended for treatment review, not automatic data cleaning approval.

dataviz.univariate.pareto_chart_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, dropna: bool = True, top_n: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Count', cumulative_label: str = 'Cumulative %', color: str | None = None, line_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive Pareto chart for categorical frequencies.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of most common categories to show.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Left y-axis label for counts.

  • cumulative_label (str) – Right y-axis label for cumulative percentage.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • line_color (str) – Cumulative percentage line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Bar.

Returns:

Interactive Pareto chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = pareto_chart_interactive(defect_types) `

Notes

The cumulative percentage trace uses the secondary y-axis.

dataviz.univariate.pareto_chart_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, dropna: bool = True, top_n: int | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str = 'Count', cumulative_label: str = 'Cumulative %', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, line_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static Pareto chart for categorical frequencies.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of most common categories to show.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Left y-axis label for counts.

  • cumulative_label (str) – Right y-axis label for cumulative percentage.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional bar color.

  • line_color (str) – Cumulative percentage line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.bar.

Returns:

Primary axes containing the Pareto bars.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = pareto_chart_static(defect_types) `

Notes

The cumulative line is drawn on a secondary y-axis.

dataviz.univariate.percentile_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Percentile', ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, marker: str = 'o', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static percentile profile plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between points.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Axes containing the percentile profile.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = percentile_plot_static(data, step=10) `

Notes

Percentile profiles provide a compact view of distribution shape.

dataviz.univariate.percentile_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Percentile', ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive percentile profile plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between points.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive percentile profile.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = percentile_plot_interactive(data, step=10) `

Notes

Percentile values are available in hover labels.

dataviz.univariate.percentile_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Percentile', ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, marker: str = 'o', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static percentile profile plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between points.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Axes containing the percentile profile.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = percentile_plot_static(data, step=10) `

Notes

Percentile profiles provide a compact view of distribution shape.

dataviz.univariate.percentile_table(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 10) → DataFrame[source]

Create a percentile table for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between rows.

Returns:

Percentile and value columns.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is not between 1 and 100 or no observations remain.

Examples

`python table = percentile_table(data, step=5) `

Notes

The table always includes 0 and 100 percentiles.

dataviz.univariate.pp_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static probability-probability plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the PP plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = pp_plot_static(data) `

Notes

PP plots compare cumulative probabilities rather than quantiles.

dataviz.univariate.pp_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive probability-probability plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive PP plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = pp_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

The 45-degree reference line represents perfect distributional agreement.

dataviz.univariate.pp_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static probability-probability plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the PP plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = pp_plot_static(data) `

Notes

PP plots compare cumulative probabilities rather than quantiles.

dataviz.univariate.qq_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static quantile-quantile plot against a theoretical distribution.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the QQ plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = qq_plot_static(data, distribution="norm") `

Notes

Points close to the reference line indicate compatibility with the selected distribution.

dataviz.univariate.qq_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive quantile-quantile plot against a theoretical distribution.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive QQ plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = qq_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Hover data exposes paired theoretical and sample quantiles.

dataviz.univariate.qq_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static quantile-quantile plot against a theoretical distribution.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the QQ plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = qq_plot_static(data, distribution="norm") `

Notes

Points close to the reference line indicate compatibility with the selected distribution.

dataviz.univariate.quality_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static bar chart of univariate quality rates.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing quality-rate bars.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the input has no observations.

Examples

`python ax = quality_bar_static(series) `

Notes

Rates are shown on the inclusive [0, 1] scale.

dataviz.univariate.quality_bar_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive bar chart of univariate quality rates.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive quality-rate chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the input has no observations.

Examples

`python fig = quality_bar_interactive(series) `

Notes

Quality rates provide a quick screen before selecting downstream visualizations.

dataviz.univariate.quality_bar_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static bar chart of univariate quality rates.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing quality-rate bars.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the input has no observations.

Examples

`python ax = quality_bar_static(series) `

Notes

Rates are shown on the inclusive [0, 1] scale.

dataviz.univariate.raincloud_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (7, 8), color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.6, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static raincloud-style plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Primary plot color.

  • alpha (float) – Point opacity.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing violin, box, and raw points.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = raincloud_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The implementation approximates a raincloud with familiar matplotlib primitives.

dataviz.univariate.raincloud_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive raincloud-style plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Primary plot color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive raincloud-style plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = raincloud_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Plotly violin traces can display violin, box, meanline, and raw points together.

dataviz.univariate.raincloud_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (7, 8), color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.6, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static raincloud-style plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Primary plot color.

  • alpha (float) – Point opacity.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing violin, box, and raw points.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = raincloud_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The implementation approximates a raincloud with familiar matplotlib primitives.

dataviz.univariate.recommended_bin_count(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['auto', 'fd', 'sturges', 'rice', 'sqrt'] = 'auto') → int[source]

Recommend a histogram bin count from common univariate rules.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (BinMethod) – Rule name: "auto", "fd", "sturges", "rice", or "sqrt".

Returns:

Recommended positive number of bins.

Return type:

int

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If method is unknown or no observations remain.

Examples

`python bins = recommended_bin_count(data, method="fd") `

Notes

"auto" chooses the larger of Freedman-Diaconis and Sturges recommendations.

dataviz.univariate.reference_band_histogram(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, band_color: str = 'gold', mean_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static histogram with mean and standard-deviation reference bands.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • band_color (str) – Reference band color.

  • mean_color (str) – Mean line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the reference-band histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = reference_band_histogram_static(data) `

Notes

The shaded band spans one sample standard deviation around the mean.

dataviz.univariate.reference_band_histogram_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, band_color: str = 'gold', mean_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram with mean and standard-deviation reference bands.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • band_color (str) – Reference band color.

  • mean_color (str) – Mean line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive reference-band histogram.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = reference_band_histogram_interactive(data) `

Notes

The standard-deviation band is represented as a translucent rectangle.

dataviz.univariate.reference_band_histogram_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, band_color: str = 'gold', mean_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static histogram with mean and standard-deviation reference bands.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • band_color (str) – Reference band color.

  • mean_color (str) – Mean line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the reference-band histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = reference_band_histogram_static(data) `

Notes

The shaded band spans one sample standard deviation around the mean.

dataviz.univariate.remove_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5) → OutlierTreatmentResult[source]

Remove observations flagged as outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule: "iqr", "zscore", or "mad".

  • threshold (float) – Threshold for z-score or MAD rules.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Original values, retained values, and mask.

Return type:

OutlierTreatmentResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If all observations are removed or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python result = remove_outliers(values) `

Notes

Removal changes sample size and should be used with care.

dataviz.univariate.resolve_univariate_data(value: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop', require_numeric: bool = False, fallback_name: str = 'Value') → UnivariateInput[source]

Resolve a column name or series-like object for univariate functions.

Parameters:

  • value (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Series-like values or a dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pandas.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe used for column-name lookup.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

  • require_numeric (bool) – Whether to coerce the resolved values to numeric.

  • fallback_name (str) – Name used for unnamed array-like values.

Returns:

Resolved values, name, inferred kind, and missing count.

Return type:

UnivariateInput

Raises:

  • TypeError – If a column name is provided without a dataframe or coercion fails.

  • ValueError – If a column is missing, missing values violate policy, or no observations remain.

Examples

`python resolved = resolve_univariate_data("sales", data=df, require_numeric=True) `

Notes

This helper is the common bridge toward consistent data= support across the module.

dataviz.univariate.resolve_weighted_series(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, weights: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Tuple[Series, Series][source]

Resolve values and weights into aligned numeric Series.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • weights (SeriesLike) – Non-negative observation weights.

Returns:

Cleaned values and aligned weights.

Return type:

tuple[pandas.Series, pandas.Series]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values or weights cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If lengths differ, weights are negative, or total weight is not positive.

Examples

`python values, weights = resolve_weighted_series([1, 2], [0.4, 0.6]) `

Notes

Rows with missing values or missing weights are removed together.

dataviz.univariate.ridgeline_plot(data: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Value', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 8), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static ridgeline-style density plot for numeric dataframe columns.

Parameters:

  • data (pandas.DataFrame) – Dataframe containing numeric columns.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Density line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing offset density curves.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data is not a dataframe.

  • ValueError – If no numeric columns are available.

Examples

`python ax = ridgeline_plot_static(df[["a", "b", "c"]]) `

Notes

This single-axes implementation avoids adding a seaborn dependency beyond package runtime.

dataviz.univariate.ridgeline_plot_interactive(data: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Value', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive ridgeline-style density plot for numeric dataframe columns.

Parameters:

  • data (pandas.DataFrame) – Dataframe containing numeric columns.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive ridgeline-style density plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data is not a dataframe.

  • ValueError – If no numeric columns are available.

Examples

`python fig = ridgeline_plot_interactive(df[["a", "b"]]) `

Notes

Violin traces provide a compact interactive ridgeline approximation.

dataviz.univariate.ridgeline_plot_static(data: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Value', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 8), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static ridgeline-style density plot for numeric dataframe columns.

Parameters:

  • data (pandas.DataFrame) – Dataframe containing numeric columns.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Density line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing offset density curves.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data is not a dataframe.

  • ValueError – If no numeric columns are available.

Examples

`python ax = ridgeline_plot_static(df[["a", "b", "c"]]) `

Notes

This single-axes implementation avoids adding a seaborn dependency beyond package runtime.

dataviz.univariate.robust_location_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, median_color: str = 'crimson', trimmed_color: str = 'darkgreen', winsor_color: str = 'darkorange', theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static histogram with robust location reference lines.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • median_color (str) – Median reference line color.

  • trimmed_color (str) – Trimmed mean reference line color.

  • winsor_color (str) – Winsorized mean reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing histogram and robust reference lines.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If robust statistics cannot be computed.

Examples

`python ax = robust_location_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The plot makes disagreements between classical and robust centers easy to see.

dataviz.univariate.robust_location_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, median_color: str = 'crimson', trimmed_color: str = 'darkgreen', winsor_color: str = 'darkorange', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram with robust location reference lines.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • median_color (str) – Median reference line color.

  • trimmed_color (str) – Trimmed mean reference line color.

  • winsor_color (str) – Winsorized mean reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive robust location chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If robust statistics cannot be computed.

Examples

`python fig = robust_location_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Reference lines are added as Plotly layout shapes.

dataviz.univariate.robust_location_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, median_color: str = 'crimson', trimmed_color: str = 'darkgreen', winsor_color: str = 'darkorange', theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static histogram with robust location reference lines.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • median_color (str) – Median reference line color.

  • trimmed_color (str) – Trimmed mean reference line color.

  • winsor_color (str) – Winsorized mean reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing histogram and robust reference lines.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If robust statistics cannot be computed.

Examples

`python ax = robust_location_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The plot makes disagreements between classical and robust centers easy to see.

dataviz.univariate.robust_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, trim_proportion: float = 0.1, winsor_limits: float = 0.05, fence_multiplier: float = 1.5) → RobustStats[source]

Compute robust summary statistics for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • trim_proportion (float) – Fraction trimmed from each tail for the trimmed mean.

  • winsor_limits (float) – Fraction winsorized from each tail for the winsorized mean.

  • fence_multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier used for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Robust center, spread, and fence summary.

Return type:

RobustStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If proportions or fence multiplier are invalid.

Examples

`python summary = robust_summary(data) `

Notes

Robust summaries complement, rather than replace, classical descriptive statistics.

dataviz.univariate.rug_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 2), color: str | None = None, height: float = 0.8, alpha: float = 0.7, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static rug plot showing every observation along an axis.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Rug line color.

  • height (float) – Rug line height in axis units.

  • alpha (float) – Rug line opacity.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the rug plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = rug_plot_static(data) `

Notes

Rug plots are most useful as companions to histograms or KDE charts.

dataviz.univariate.rug_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 250, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive rug plot showing every observation along an axis.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Rug marker color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive rug plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = rug_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Interactive rugs use tick markers so individual points remain inspectable.

dataviz.univariate.rug_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 2), color: str | None = None, height: float = 0.8, alpha: float = 0.7, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static rug plot showing every observation along an axis.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Rug line color.

  • height (float) – Rug line height in axis units.

  • alpha (float) – Rug line opacity.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the rug plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = rug_plot_static(data) `

Notes

Rug plots are most useful as companions to histograms or KDE charts.

dataviz.univariate.sentinel_rate(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, sentinels: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → float[source]

Compute the rate of values matching configured sentinels.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • sentinels (SeriesLike) – Values that should be treated as sentinel markers.

Returns:

Share of observations matching any sentinel value.

Return type:

float

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no observations or sentinels are provided.

Examples

`python rate = sentinel_rate(values, [-999]) `

Notes

This helper catches common data-quality placeholders that are not missing values.

dataviz.univariate.sentinel_value_counts(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, sentinels: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Series[source]

Count configured sentinel values in one variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • sentinels (SeriesLike) – Values that should be treated as sentinel markers.

Returns:

Counts indexed by sentinel value.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no sentinel values are provided.

Examples

`python counts = sentinel_value_counts(values, [-999, "N/A"]) `

Notes

Sentinel values are counted before any type coercion.

dataviz.univariate.string_length_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Series[source]

Compute string lengths for text-like observations.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input text-like observations.

Returns:

String lengths for non-missing observations.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python lengths = string_length_summary(["alpha", "beta"]) `

Notes

Values are converted to strings before measuring length.

dataviz.univariate.strip_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (6, 8), color: str | None = None, jitter: float = 0.08, alpha: float = 0.7, seed: int | None = 0, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static jittered strip plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Marker color.

  • jitter (float) – Horizontal jitter magnitude.

  • alpha (float) – Marker opacity.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducible jitter.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the strip plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = strip_plot_static(data) `

Notes

Strip plots expose individual observations without binning.

dataviz.univariate.strip_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, jitter: float = 0.08, seed: int | None = 0, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 600) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive jittered strip plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Marker color.

  • jitter (float) – Horizontal jitter magnitude.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducible jitter.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive strip plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = strip_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Hover labels expose each cleaned observation value.

dataviz.univariate.strip_plot_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (6, 8), color: str | None = None, jitter: float = 0.08, alpha: float = 0.7, seed: int | None = 0, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static jittered strip plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Marker color.

  • jitter (float) – Horizontal jitter magnitude.

  • alpha (float) – Marker opacity.

  • seed (Optional[int]) – Random seed for reproducible jitter.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the strip plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = strip_plot_static(data) `

Notes

Strip plots expose individual observations without binning.

dataviz.univariate.survival_curve(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static empirical survival curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the survival curve.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = survival_curve_static(data) `

Notes

Survival curves emphasize upper-tail probability.

dataviz.univariate.survival_curve_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive empirical survival curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive survival curve.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = survival_curve_interactive(data) `

Notes

The line uses horizontal-vertical steps for empirical survival.

dataviz.univariate.survival_curve_static(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static empirical survival curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the survival curve.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = survival_curve_static(data) `

Notes

Survival curves emphasize upper-tail probability.

dataviz.univariate.survival_values(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute empirical survival curve values.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

Returns:

Sorted values and survival probabilities.

Return type:

tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python x, survival = survival_values(data) `

Notes

Survival probability is estimated as 1 - ECDF with rank-based plotting positions.

dataviz.univariate.token_count_summary(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Series[source]

Compute whitespace token counts for text-like observations.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input text-like observations.

Returns:

Token counts for non-missing observations.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no non-missing observations remain.

Examples

`python counts = token_count_summary(["hello world", "one"]) `

Notes

Empty strings produce zero tokens.

dataviz.univariate.top_terms(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, top_n: int = 20, lowercase: bool = True) → Series[source]

Count the most common whitespace-delimited terms.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input text-like observations.

  • top_n (int) – Number of terms to return.

  • lowercase (bool) – Whether to lowercase text before tokenization.

Returns:

Term counts indexed by term.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If top_n is invalid or no terms are found.

Examples

`python terms = top_terms(["red blue", "red"], top_n=5) `

Notes

This lightweight helper is intentionally dependency-free and not a full NLP tokenizer.

dataviz.univariate.top_terms_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, top_n: int = 20, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static bar chart of common text terms.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input text-like observations.

  • top_n (int) – Number of terms to show.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing top-term counts.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no terms are found or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = top_terms_bar_static(messages) `

Notes

Use this for lightweight exploratory text profiling.
